nodes.title: Use a Set to track cleanup callbacks
nodes.why: Set gives fast add/remove and prevents duplicate references from piling up.
nodes.tradeoff: Set is cleaner for membership operations, while arrays are simpler but slower to remove.

nodes.title: Keep a disposed flag
nodes.why: The flag enforces one-way lifecycle: active -> disposed.
nodes.alternative: Infer disposed state only from set size.
nodes.tradeoff: Explicit flag is clearer and avoids edge cases when set is empty but still active.

nodes.title: Validate add input is a function
nodes.why: Failing early avoids silent bugs from invalid cleanup entries.
nodes.tradeoff: Strict validation is safer, but throws if callers misuse the API.

nodes.title: Run cleanup immediately if already disposed
nodes.why: This prevents leaks when callers add cleanup too late.
nodes.tradeoff: Immediate run is pragmatic and safe, but can surprise callers expecting deferred behavior.

nodes.title: Make returned remover idempotent
nodes.why: Calling the remover multiple times should be harmless.
nodes.alternative: Allow repeated deletes with no guard.
nodes.tradeoff: A tiny flag avoids repeated work and keeps semantics explicit.

nodes.title: Make dispose itself idempotent
nodes.why: Multiple dispose calls are common in real apps and should be safe.
nodes.tradeoff: No-op on repeat is easier for callers, but hides repeated lifecycle calls.

nodes.title: Run each cleanup with guaranteed removal
nodes.why: Even if a cleanup throws, it still gets removed so bag state stays consistent.
nodes.alternative: Delete before calling fn or skip finally.
nodes.tradeoff: Finally keeps state robust, while still letting cleanup errors propagate.

nodes.title: Expose current active cleanup count
nodes.why: `size()` gives quick visibility into whether resources are still registered.
nodes.tradeoff: Helpful for debugging, but minor extra API surface.
